info.set_name: Reproductive Comprehensive Set 1: Sexual Differentation (Short Answer)

q1.prompt: What is the fundamental determinant of genetic sex in a developing human embryo, and at what point is it established?
correctOptionIds: Genetic sex is determined by the sex chromosomes, either XX or XY, and is established at the moment of fertilization.

q2.prompt: Which specific gene located on the short arm of the Y chromosome is responsible for initiating the development of the gonads into testes?
correctOptionIds: The SRY gene (Sex-determining Region of the Y chromosome).

q3.prompt: In the absence of the SRY gene, into which structure will the bipotential undifferentiated gonad naturally develop?
correctOptionIds: The undifferentiated gonads will develop into ovaries.

q4.prompt: Identify the two specific cell types that differentiate within the fetal testes and name the primary hormone each one secretes during the first trimester.
correctOptionIds: Sertoli cells (secreting Antimüllerian Hormone) and Leydig cells (secreting Testosterone).

q5.prompt: What is the specific physiologic role of Antimüllerian Hormone (AMH), also known as Müllerian Inhibiting Substance (MIS), in male phenotypic differentiation?
correctOptionIds: AMH causes the atrophy and regression of the Müllerian ducts.

q6.prompt: Which embryonic duct system is the precursor to the female internal reproductive tract, and what are the specific structures it becomes?
correctOptionIds: The Müllerian ducts; they become the fallopian tubes, uterus, and upper one-third of the vagina.

q7.prompt: Which embryonic duct system is the precursor to the male internal reproductive tract, and what three structures does it form under the influence of testosterone?
correctOptionIds: The Wolffian ducts; they become the epididymis, vas deferens, and seminal vesicles.

q8.prompt: Explain why the internal female reproductive organs do not develop in a normal male fetus, even though the genetic "default" is female.
correctOptionIds: The secretion of AMH by the fetal Sertoli cells actively suppresses the development of the Müllerian ducts.

q9.prompt: Which androgen is specifically required for the differentiation of the male external genitalia, such as the penis and the scrotum?

q10.prompt: Name the enzyme responsible for the conversion of testosterone to its more potent derivative in the target tissues of the external genitalia.

q11.prompt: If a fetus is genetically male (XY) but the testes fail to produce any hormones, what will be the resulting internal and external phenotype?
correctOptionIds: The individual will have a female phenotype (female external genitalia and internal Müllerian structures) because neither testosterone nor AMH was present to promote male structures or suppress female ones.

q12.prompt: In the development of a genetic female (XX), why do the Wolffian ducts normally regress?
correctOptionIds: They regress because of the absence of testosterone, which is required for their growth and differentiation.

q13.prompt: Define "gonadal sex" and explain how it differs from "phenotypic sex."
correctOptionIds: Gonadal sex refers to whether the person has testes or ovaries; phenotypic sex refers to the physical appearance of the internal and external genitalia.

q14.prompt: What is the clinical result of a deficiency in the enzyme 5α-reductase regarding the external appearance of a genetic male at birth?
correctOptionIds: The individual will have normal male internal genitalia (Wolffian derivatives) but will have female-like or ambiguous external genitalia because DHT was not available during development.

q15.prompt: Describe the condition of Androgen Insensitivity Syndrome (Testicular Feminization) in terms of genotype, the presence of gonads, and the resulting external physical appearance.
correctOptionIds: The genotype is 46, XY; testes are present (usually intra-abdominal); the external appearance is female due to the lack of androgen receptors in target tissues.
